displayTitle: How Much of the Coil the Part Fills

beats.narration: Encircling and bobbin coils have no lift-off to chase. Their coupling is pure geometry, and one number captures it: fill factor.

payload.title: FILL FACTOR
payload.sub: How much of the coil's field actually reaches the part.

beats.narration: Fill factor is an area ratio, so it collapses to diameter ratio squared. A nineteen-millimeter bar in a twenty-one-millimeter coil gives zero point eight two.

payload.heading: Diameter ratio, squared
payload.note: ENCIRCLING: (d/D)²  ·  BOBBIN: (probe/tube ID)²  ·  AIR ANNULUS = LOST COUPLING

beats.narration: Two habits separate a Level Two from a guess: use the effective coil diameter, and for bobbins use tube inside diameter — never the outside.
Amplitude rides on fill factor. On the impedance plane, falling coupling slides the dot straight toward the air point — the feed-through twin of the lift-off line.

payload.heading: What lower fill factor costs you

beats.narration: So specify the snuggest coil the product can safely feed — about point seven-five to point nine — and whenever coil or probe size changes, re-standardize.

payload.sub: New geometry means new coupling — and a new setup.
